quotes.verbatim_quote:
To conserve space the following studies will have an upper,
middle, lower voice change every four keys of the cycle of
fifths instead of the usual twelve. However, the fingering for
all of the keys will be represented in this way:
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the chapter's compressed notation—one voice motion type covers four cycle-of-fifths keys—which the reader must decode to access all twelve keys.

quotes.topic: Step loss compensated at range edges
quotes.verbatim_quote:
‘When a step is lost on the low end because of running out of
fingerboard a step is always added on the high end, and vice
versa.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the range-compensation rule that lets the reader reconstruct full-scale studies even when fingerboard limits truncate an end of the written exercise.

quotes.verbatim_quote: The 2nd inversion lower voice super is the same notation as root triads lower voice sub, but the tonic step is in a different place-take the above G scale as an example: When applying it as a root scale the 3rd step is the tonic chord=D-B-G- therefore, 3 becomes 1-this applies to all keys Major or Minor
quotes.why_load_bearing: Demonstrates how identical written notation must be re-labeled based on which inversion is the conceptual 'origin'—the practical instance of the cause/effect rule from chapter 8.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In root triads most of the reaches are quite great. However,
these voicings must be included no matter how impractical
they may appear, because by design they belong to the family
of basic triads. In other words, the voice motion cannot be
ignored just because the reaches become extended. The same
treatment for all the triads is necessary in order to keep the
continuity of the concept—what is done to one must be done
to the others.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Justifies including impractical-reach root voicings on conceptual-symmetry grounds, defending the completeness of the super/sub system over playability.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The difference between an original notation and a compro-
mise fingering is noticeable harmonically when the tones are
sustained, but when the moving voice is detached and
sounded separately there is no notation difference.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the audible test for when compromise fingerings are acceptable substitutes—detached articulation hides the difference, sustained voicings reveal it.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Remember to always transpose slowly, correctly. Also, when transposing these studies adhere to the cycle of fifths to
keep the continuity.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Prescribes the cycle-of-fifths transposition order that preserves the chapter's pedagogical continuity across the twelve keys not explicitly written.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Practice all scale forms, major and minor, first station to first staion, second station to second station, third station to
third station, etc. Both directions, up, and down.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the station-matched practice pattern that drills positional consistency across the scale, distinct from straight ascending/descending runs.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This pattern helps to build mental agility while building physical dexterity. The ability to be able to pick up a
phrase or a progression (etc.) an octave higher or lower is a golden asset
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names the high-value skill—on-the-fly octave displacement—that the station-to-station practice format is designed to develop.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Many times individual fingers are used where double stops and small barres could be employed; when voice motion continues, double stops wont work - practise both ways-mix fingerings and sets - change cross over points etc.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains why individual-finger fingerings are preferred over barres in open-voicing contexts where voice motion is ongoing—barres lock voices that need to move.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Variation - Exercises such as this develop independent finger control - study carefully
quotes.why_load_bearing: Calls out the chapter's super/sub variation exercises as targeted independence drills rather than mere voicing catalogs.
